OK, Now I see what this is better.  Here is the code I would use to compute it without having to worry about passing over due north:
```
# Go some reasonable distance from crval in the +y direction.  Say, 1 degree.
plusy = world_pos.deproject(galsim.PositionD(u/galsim.arcsec, v/galsim.arcsec + 3600),
                               projection='gnomonic')
# Find the angle between this point, crval and due north
north = galsim.CelestialCoord(0.*galsim.degrees, 90.*galsim.degrees)
pa_sca = sca_tp_rot - crval.angleBetween(plusy, north)
```

`LiveImage` now has two options.

1. It can continue to work the way it does today, ignoring the new documents (datum and resource) that flow through and trading tokens for arrays via a `Broker`. A downside of this approach is that sometimes the RunEngine hands `LiveImage` an Event with some datum_id _before_ the `Registry` knows about it. (MongoDB takes some time to reflect new information in queries.) To be robust, `LiveImage` has to be prepared to poll `Broker` about a `datum_id` more than once, waiting for things to sync.
2. Alternatively, `LiveImage` can cache the datum and resource documents and use a Handler directly to load the data. In this scenario, `LiveImage` doesn't need a `Broker` (there doesn't even technically need to _be_ one!), but it does now need a Handler.

and

I know, but this is a rather inconvenient stylistic choice. When a comment is on the same line as the code it comments on, there's no ambiguity which line of code it corresponds to.  When the comment is on its own line, it could correspond to the line before or the line after. It's only by convention that you know it's the latter.
It makes sense for header files, when we expect doxygen to generate API documentation, but why have the same constraint in the rest of the code, where comments are rather for the developers reading the code?
